summary: Chinese author, considered the father of modern Chinese literature. Known for 'A Madman's Diary,' 'The True Story of Ah Q,' 'Medicine,' and the story collections Call to Arms and Wandering. Wrote in vernacular Chinese (baihua).

works.name: A Madman's Diary
works.description: Short story with a narrator who believes everyone around him is a cannibal; first major work of modern Chinese literature written in vernacular.

clues.clue: Narrator believes everyone is a cannibal; reads history and finds 'Eat People' written between the lines; first major vernacular Chinese literary work

ends with the plea 'Save the children'

works.name: The True Story of Ah Q
works.description: Novella about a village bully who practices 'spiritual victories' to rationalize defeats.

clues.clue: Ah Q practices 'spiritual victories' — rationalizes every defeat as a moral victory; executed during revolution

comprehensive_summary:
Lu Xun (1881-1936) is considered the father of modern Chinese literature. He abandoned a medical career after watching a lantern slide of a Chinese man about to be executed, deciding to heal China's spirit through literature. He wrote in vernacular Chinese (baihua).

'A Madman's Diary' is the first major work of modern Chinese literature in vernacular. Its narrator believes everyone around him is a cannibal and reads history only to find 'Eat People' written between the lines. It ends with the plea 'Save the children.' 'The True Story of Ah Q' follows a village bully who practices 'spiritual victories,' rationalizing every defeat as a moral win, before being executed during a revolution. 'Medicine' features a father buying a blood-soaked bread roll as a tuberculosis cure, the blood coming from an executed revolutionary. His story collections include Call to Arms and Wandering.
recursive_suggestions: The True Story of Ah Q
